author: @scantrain5007
text:
Dear Rob,
in Germany it's called "Thermo-Element". I been thinking about this to install it in a funnel from heating kettle to get at least a little energy which goes normally to the birds. 

Your printed thermoelectric generator is for lower temperatures off course; I guess up to 100°C only. In winter time the difference between outside and inside a house might (depend on which country / area) go up to 20°C or more. In this way there should be a potential to get the one or other WH for free. And in your way very simple to make!

author: @Thrive-Off-Grid
text: Print this on Silica Cloth, this cloth can handle 1800*f. If coat the cells with Sodium Silicate as high temp electrolyte between the materials, you then create a combination of both a battery and a printable TEG. You may see a huge boost in output and may note they will hold a charge. You may even find you can charge the cells directly from the heat source.  I did this same thing but used aluminum oxide very fine powder as the cathode. I took this video out of hibernation check it out. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tzgA8JgibUQ

replies.author: @Thrive-Off-Grid
replies.text: If we can get the silica cloth to act like a wick, we can keep the sodium silicate supplied with water to keep wet. Once the water is gone the electrolyte quits working.  This is why I think there is something going on with the H being liberated and processed like a fuel cell.

replies.author: @Thrive-Off-Grid
replies.text: I was able to achieve 6 volts out of my cells and were very crude. In fact the very first ones were just a smaller scale of exactly what you did here.

replies.author: @Thrive-Off-Grid
replies.text: The cell in the video I coated the silica cloth with sodium silicate first. Then used it again for the binder for both the Aluminum oxide and the graphite powder. In the video the tegs modules you see I had bought previously and are what got me going  in this. But those modules are broken in the video and are not the source of power. I was just using them to isolate the vice grips from the anode and cathode of the cell.

replies.author: @Thrive-Off-Grid
replies.text: one last thing, I tried the peltier effect, and this is  how I discovered the energy storage capability. The cell did seem to cool but not drastic, I was hitting them with 12 volts.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I did check it out mate - very interesting - what was the anode?

author: @wanderingzanzey2126
text:
This is pretty cool, similar to the Lovell monotherm video you did - but you are using the Seebeck effect here. NiCr based inks (powder + polyaniline and etOH) work better as the Ni source than Ni itself. Or even Ni and Ag work really well as the Ni electrode. I have done some experiments in this area myself, with carbon dispersions. I found that a Carbon paint in a junction with a NiCr paint actually performed well enough to take reasonably accurate temperature measurements with them. I used polyan and the target metal to make the ink - then i sieved it and used a Thermocouple template i found online and made a stencil. I then airbrushed the paints. They were 40mm x 40mm and had an average resistance of 0.35ohms/square. I was able to hook the painted tracks to my Arduino and use them as a two-wire temperature sensor. Was pretty cool :) I did this a few years back. Temperature sensing wasn't my main goal at the time - creating a paintable peltier device was - so i quickly moved away from using Carbon as the reference electrode material and onto TaBi chalcogenide mixtures. They are pretty cheap and have amazing Seebeck coefficients (they are the materials current peltier elements are made out of). I have been able to get 10°C movement across the paint i came up with. I am still tinkering though. 

Love the video :) There is so much potential in a cheap waste-heat harvesting system. Even it just fr iOT at first - huge potential in paintables.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lot of interesting stuff in your post mate - I'll dig into a bit more for sure - not sure if I want to use tantalum based inks - I might persist with the carbon a while longer - stubborn - you know that lol - but I agree printable are a very interesting area

author: @AndreasGosch
text:
Question technical application
The performance of solar panels drops in the summer months because they heat up. I've been looking for a solution for a long time. I want to dissipate the heat to increase efficiency and generate extra electricity for my balcony power plant.
So far I was thinking about Pelitier elements and water cooling.
But a corresponding painting of the back would be a great thing. Stick passive heatsinks on top to get a higher delta temperature.
Combined with a DIY zinc bromine flow battery and you come very close to the offgrid. For little money.

author: @technosaurus3805
text:
Does anyone know a good hackable inkjet that could have the color inks replaced with carbon, boron carbide and carbon nitride water based inks and the black ink replaced with a non-water-soluble insulating material.  Preferably with a  way to calibrate it to run the same sheet multiple times (perhaps a cheap plotter?)

This could be a game changer for printable circuit prototyping and a modified silk screen process could be useful for medium volume production.

It would also be kinda cool to use a non-modified printer for visualization purposes.

BTW - the boron and nitride are to form P-N junctions for carbon base ICs.  The multiple layers is not only useful for multilayer circuits but necessary for printable transformers.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: There are several CISS systems around mate - worth looking into that - if you want to hack something choose a piezo based inkjet not a heat based device - I think epson does them but you will need to check

author: @michaelhayes7849
text:
Thanks, Rob. I've been waiting for you to do this. Brilliant! 😊

There is a metal used in magnetic refrigeration that may couple well with your TC.

https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Magnetic_refrigeration

https://www.rwmmint.com/products/gadolinium-gd

A small slug of Gd cost around $80 yet supplies are sketchy.

Using a strong perminate magnet, the Gd slug can pump heat or cold. Your TC can possibly be given a strong delta T with simple moving parts.

https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=magnetic+Refrigeration,+Gd&hl=en&as_sdt=0&as_vis=1&oi=scholart

replies.author: @michaelhayes7849
replies.text:
It now comes down to creating a strong thermal difference, or differential, in a niffty way. 

Let's say a thin wheel rim had the TC painted on the inside of the rim with magnets attached around the bottom outside of the rim. By placing a slug of Gd in the wheel rim and letting simple oscillations of the Gd slug heat/cool the inside rim which is the magnet/TC.

It could make for a niffty table top work of art or much more.

replies.author: @AtlasReburdened
replies.text: Umm, he just did....  you can use special materials to enhance either the Peltier or Seebeck effect but they're both thermoelectrics and most schema that display thermoelectric conversion are thermodynamically reversible. So TECs are low quality TEGs and TEGs are low quality TECs.

replies.author: @earlyriser438
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering ain't that the truth. 

Sturgill Simpson said it well:

"Bein' pulled a million ways all at the same time
It's enough to make anyone go insane
When you find yourself forgetting all your own rhymes
And givin' up on dreams floating 'round inside your brain"

At some point you have to hit mute and be the best clockmaker on Mars.

replies.author: @philipvernejules9926
replies.text: ......peltier module,,, do you mean for electric coldness production as in refrigeration? Those devices require materials that conduct electricity but not heat and it's semiconductors are what fit those specifications. Semiconductors are crystal based things , it gets to be specialized.
